name: fast_rcnn

members.name: FastRCNNConfig
members.signature: FastRCNNConfig(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, roi_size: int = 7, spatial_scale: float = 1.0 / 16.0, dropout: float = 0.5, bbox_reg_weights: tuple[float, float, float, float] = (10.0, 10.0, 5.0, 5.0), score_thresh: float = 0.05, nms_thresh: float = 0.5, max_detections: int = 300)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, roi_size: int = 7, spatial_scale: float = 1.0 / 16.0, dropout: float = 0.5, bbox_reg_weights: tuple[float, float, float, float] = (10.0, 10.0, 5.0, 5.0), score_thresh: float = 0.05, nms_thresh: float = 0.5, max_detections: int = 300)

members.extended:
Fast R-CNN (Girshick, ICCV 2015) runs the CNN on the **full image once**,
then extracts per-proposal features with RoI Pooling on the shared feature
map.  This is the key advance over R-CNN (one forward pass vs one per
proposal).

Default backbone: VGG16 conv layers (conv1_1 … conv5_3, pool5 removed).
The feature map stride is 16 (four max-pool layers before pool5).

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Foreground object classes.  Background is class 0,
             giving (num_classes + 1) output logits.

parameters.name: spatial_scale
parameters.description:
Ratio of feature-map size to input image size.
             For VGG16 with pool5 removed: 1/16.

parameters.name: dropout
parameters.description: Dropout probability after fc6 and fc7.

parameters.name: bbox_reg_weights
parameters.description:
Per-component weight scaling for bbox regression
              targets (tx, ty, tw, th).  Matches the paper's
              normalisation by (0, 0, 0, 0) mean / (0.1, 0.1,
              0.2, 0.2) std, encoded here as multiplicative
              weights.

parameters.name: score_thresh
parameters.description: Minimum class score at inference time.

members.name: FastRCNNForObjectDetection

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, proposals: list[Tensor] | None = None, targets: list[dict[str, Tensor]] | None = None)
methods.summary: Run Fast R-CNN on a batch of images.

parameters.name: proposals
parameters.description: list of B tensors, each (N_i, 4) xyxy proposals.

parameters.name: targets
parameters.description:
Optional training targets (list of dicts with
       "boxes" and "labels").

returns.description:
`ObjectDetectionOutput`:
`logits`     : (Σ N_i, num_classes + 1) raw class logits.
`pred_boxes` : (Σ N_i, 4) decoded xyxy top-class boxes.
`loss`       : scalar multi-task loss (only with targets).

methods.name: postprocess
methods.summary: Apply per-class NMS to raw Fast R-CNN output.

parameters.name: proposals
parameters.description: Proposal list passed to `forward()` (for shape info).

returns.description:
Per-image list of result dicts:
`"boxes"`  : (K_det, 4)  kept xyxy detections
`"scores"` : (K_det,)    class confidence scores
`"labels"` : (K_det,)    class indices (1-based)

members.extended:
The successor to R-CNN that fixes its main bottleneck: rather than running
the CNN once *per proposal*, the backbone is applied **once** to the whole
image, and per-proposal features are extracted via `roi_pool` over
the shared feature map (7x7 at stride 16 for the VGG16 default).  The
pooled tensor is flattened, passed through two FC layers
(`fc6`, `fc7`), and split into sibling class (`cls_score`) and
class-specific bounding-box (`bbox_pred`) heads.  At training time the
model computes the paper's multi-task loss

$$
L = L_{\mathrm{cls}} + \lambda\, L_{\mathrm{loc}},
$$

with $L_{\mathrm{cls}}$ the categorical cross-entropy and
$L_{\mathrm{loc}}$ the smooth-$L_1$ regression loss applied
only to foreground proposals.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the `fast_rcnn` factory for
the paper-cited VGG16 configuration (RoI 7x7, stride-16 feature
map, 80 COCO classes).

examples:
Inference with externally-supplied proposals:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.fast_rcnn import fast_rcnn
>>> model = fast_rcnn(num_classes=20)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 800)
>>> proposals = [lucid.tensor(
...     [[10.0, 10.0, 200.0, 200.0],
...      [50.0, 60.0, 300.0, 280.0]])]
>>> out = model(x, proposals)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 21)
>>> out.loss is None
True
Training with ground-truth targets to compute the multi-task loss:
>>> targets = [{
...     "boxes":  lucid.tensor([[20.0, 20.0, 180.0, 180.0]]),
...     "labels": lucid.tensor([3], dtype=lucid.int64),
... }]
>>> out = model(x, proposals, targets=targets)
>>> out.loss.shape
()
notes:
See Girshick, "Fast R-CNN", ICCV 2015 ([arXiv:1504.08083](https://arxiv.org/abs/1504.08083)).  Bounding-box
targets follow the paper's parameterisation



$$
t_x = w_x \frac{G_x - P_x}{P_w},\quad
t_y = w_y \frac{G_y - P_y}{P_h},\quad
t_w = w_w \log\!\frac{G_w}{P_w},\quad
t_h = w_h \log\!\frac{G_h}{P_h},
$$

with the default normalisation weights $(w_x, w_y, w_w, w_h) = (10, 10, 5, 5)$ matching the Fast R-CNN Caffe reference.  RoI Pool (not
RoI Align) is used to preserve faithfulness — see `MaskRCNNForObjectDetection`
for the RoI Align successor.  Per-class boxes are decoded for all classes
and per-class NMS is applied by `postprocess`.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description:
VGG16 conv1_1 .. conv5_3 trunk (pool5 omitted) producing a
$(B, 512, H/16, W/16)$ feature map.

attributes.name: roi_head
attributes.description:
RoI feature processor: flatten -> `fc6` (4096) -> `fc7` (4096)
-> sibling `cls_score` (K + 1 logits) and `bbox_pred`
(4K class-specific deltas).

members.name: fast_rcnn
members.summary: Fast R-CNN with VGG16 backbone (Girshick, ICCV 2015).
members.extended:
Builds a `FastRCNNForObjectDetection` with the paper-cited
VGG16 topology: shared backbone applied once to the full image, then
7x7 RoI Pool over the stride-16 feature map followed by two 4096-dim
FC layers and sibling K+1 / 4K heads.  Approximately 138M parameters
(most in the FC layers) and ~146x faster training than R-CNN while
reaching the same VOC2007 mAP of 66.9% (paper Table 1).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

returns.description:
Detector with the Fast R-CNN VGG16 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.fast_rcnn import fast_rcnn
>>> model = fast_rcnn(num_classes=80)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 800)
>>> proposals = [lucid.tensor(
...     [[10.0, 10.0, 200.0, 200.0],
...      [50.0, 60.0, 300.0, 280.0]])]
>>> out = model(x, proposals)
>>> out.logits.shape, out.pred_boxes.shape
((2, 81), (2, 80, 4))
notes:
See Girshick, "Fast R-CNN", ICCV 2015 ([arXiv:1504.08083](https://arxiv.org/abs/1504.08083)).  Region
proposals must still be supplied externally — the Faster R-CNN
successor folds proposal generation into the network via the Region
Proposal Network (RPN).  The default `spatial_scale = 1/16` matches
VGG16's four max-pool layers before pool5.

citation: Girshick, Ross. "Fast R-CNN." Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision, 2015, pp. 1440–1448.
theory:
Fast R-CNN closes the dominant performance gap of R-CNN — the
one-CNN-forward-per-proposal cost — by sharing convolutional
computation across all proposals in an image.

The CNN is run **once** on the full image, producing a feature
map at stride $s$ (e.g. $s = 16$ for VGG-16).  For
each region proposal, **RoI Pooling** extracts a fixed-size
$H \times W$ (paper: $7 \times 7$) sub-window from
the shared feature map by quantising the proposal coordinates to
the feature grid and max-pooling adaptively inside each cell.

The pooled features feed two FC layers and **two sibling output
heads**:



$$
L(p, u, t^u, v) = L_{\mathrm{cls}}(p, u)
    + \lambda \, [u \ge 1] \, L_{\mathrm{loc}}(t^u, v),
$$

where $L_{\mathrm{cls}}$ is softmax log-loss over
$K+1$ classes and $L_{\mathrm{loc}}$ is the smooth
$\ell_1$ regression loss on the parameterised offsets
$t^u$ for the predicted class $u$.  The Iverson
bracket $[u \ge 1]$ disables regression on background RoIs.

Beyond the speedup (≈9× training, ≈213× inference over R-CNN),
Fast R-CNN replaces the SVM + post-hoc regressor with a single
multi-task network, which **improves accuracy** because gradients
from both heads jointly tune the shared trunk.
